question: Consider the three-state Potts model on a two-dimensional square lattice with quenched nonmagnetic disorder at spin concentration p=0.95 (fraction p of lattice sites carry a spin; the remaining 1-p are nonmagnetic vacancies). The ferromagnetic exchange coupling is J > 0 (Boltzmann constant k_B = 1). A Monte Carlo simulation is performed using finite-size scaling (FSS) to extract static critical exponents at the ferromagnetic phase transition. FSS uses log-log fits of (i) the order-parameter magnetization M_L, (ii) the susceptibility chi_L, and (iii) the derivative of the Binder cumulant U'_L, each as a function of linear system size L at the estimated critical point. Report the following critical exponents as a JSON object with keys: "nu" (correlation-length exponent, xi ~ |T-T_c|^{-nu}), "alpha" (specific-heat exponent, C ~ |T-T_c|^{-alpha}), "gamma" (susceptibility exponent, chi ~ |T-T_c|^{-gamma}), "beta" (magnetization exponent, M ~ |T-T_c|^beta). Each value should be a float. Do not include T_c.

metadata.system: Three-state Potts model, square lattice, dilution p=0.95
metadata.source_excerpt: the static critical exponents are nu = 0.84(1) (correlation-length exponent), alpha = 0.30(1) (specific-heat exponent), gamma = 1.45(1) (susceptibility exponent), and beta = 0.12(1) (magnetization exponent).
metadata.difficulty_note: Crossover regime between pure and diluted universality classes; exponents are intermediate.

question: Consider the three-state Potts model on a two-dimensional square lattice with quenched nonmagnetic disorder at spin concentration p=0.90 (fraction p of lattice sites carry a spin; the remaining 1-p are nonmagnetic vacancies). The ferromagnetic exchange coupling is J > 0. A Monte Carlo simulation uses finite-size scaling (FSS) — log-log fits of the magnetization M_L, susceptibility chi_L, and Binder-cumulant derivative U'_L versus system size L — to extract static critical exponents at the ferromagnetic transition. Report the following critical exponents as a JSON object with keys: "nu" (correlation-length exponent), "alpha" (specific-heat exponent), "gamma" (susceptibility exponent), "beta" (magnetization exponent). Each value should be a float.

metadata.system: Three-state Potts model, square lattice, dilution p=0.90
metadata.source_excerpt: the static critical exponents are nu = 1.02(1) (correlation-length exponent), alpha = 0.12(1) (specific-heat exponent), gamma = 1.55(1) (susceptibility exponent), and beta = 0.17(1) (magnetization exponent).
metadata.difficulty_note: Well into the diluted universality class; Harris criterion activated (alpha>0 for pure model).

cold_solve.probe_model: claude-sonnet baseline (a cloud HPC platform sandbox for C1)

metadata.gold_dispute: baseline gives random-fixed-point exponents (nu=1.17,alpha=-0.34) vs gold (nu=1.02,alpha=+0.12); gold may be the paper measured values at p=0.90 (crossover) vs asymptotic random FP; adjudicate

question: Consider the three-state Potts model on a two-dimensional square lattice with quenched nonmagnetic disorder at spin concentration p=0.80 (fraction p of lattice sites carry a spin; the remaining 1-p are nonmagnetic vacancies). The ferromagnetic exchange coupling is J > 0. A Monte Carlo simulation uses finite-size scaling (FSS) — log-log fits of the magnetization M_L, susceptibility chi_L, and Binder-cumulant derivative U'_L versus system size L — to extract static critical exponents at the ferromagnetic transition. Report the following critical exponents as a JSON object with keys: "nu" (correlation-length exponent), "alpha" (specific-heat exponent), "gamma" (susceptibility exponent), "beta" (magnetization exponent). Each value should be a float.

metadata.system: Three-state Potts model, square lattice, dilution p=0.80
metadata.source_excerpt: the static critical exponents are nu = 1.03(3) (correlation-length exponent), alpha = 0.11(3) (specific-heat exponent), gamma = 1.57(3) (susceptibility exponent), and beta = 0.16(3) (magnetization exponent).
metadata.difficulty_note: Strong dilution; larger FSS errors (3 in last digit). Converged to diluted fixed point.

question: A spatial evolutionary game on a two-dimensional square lattice (N = L^2 sites, periodic boundary conditions) is mapped to an antiferromagnetic Ising-like model with a staggered-magnetization order parameter m_s. Agent strategies S_i in {+1,-1} evolve via single-site self-questioning updates at effective social temperature T = 0.02, along the parameter path a = (1/2)p + 0.4 in the weak Prisoner's Dilemma parameterization. Finite-size scaling (FSS) is performed at two second-order (continuous) antiferromagnetic critical points located at p_c^{(1)} = 0.01127 +/- 0.00014 and p_c^{(2)} = 0.13156 +/- 0.00008 using: (i) log-log fits of m_L(p_c) vs L to get beta/nu (magnetization-to-correlation-length exponent ratio), (ii) log-log fits of chi_L(p_c) vs L (chi = N[<m_s^2> - <m_s>^2]) to get gamma/nu (susceptibility-to-correlation-length exponent ratio), (iii) log-log fits of U'_L(p_c) vs L (Binder cumulant derivative) to get 1/nu (inverse correlation-length exponent). At p_c^{(1)} = 0.01127, report as a JSON object with keys: "beta_over_nu" (beta/nu ratio), "gamma_over_nu" (gamma/nu ratio), "nu" (correlation-length exponent, obtained as 1 / (1/nu) from the fit). Each value should be a float.

metadata.system: Spatial evolutionary game (weak PD) on square lattice, mapped to antiferromagnetic Ising; T=0.02, path a=(1/2)p+0.4, left critical point p_c^{(1)}=0.01127
metadata.source_excerpt: at p_c^{(1)} = 0.01127, beta/nu = 0.116 +/- 0.0093, gamma/nu = 1.749 +/- 0.0212, and 1/nu = 0.977 +/- 0.0391; within uncertainties these agree with 2D Ising values (beta/nu ~ 1/8, gamma/nu ~ 7/4, nu ~ 1).
metadata.difficulty_note: Ising universality class for a game-theoretic lattice model; exponent ratios directly measured via FSS log-log fits.

question: Consider the three-dimensional XY lattice model on a simple cubic lattice with quenched columnar (line) disorder: in-plane couplings J_{i,x} and J_{i,y} are drawn uniformly from {0.1, 1.9} for each site column, while z-couplings are uniform J_{i,z} = 1. The model describes the superconducting transition in a material with parallel columnar defects. An anisotropic finite-size scaling (FSS) analysis is performed with aspect ratio L_z ~ L^{zeta}, where zeta is the anisotropy exponent (ratio of parallel to perpendicular correlation-length exponents). From Monte Carlo FSS using the Binder ratio g = 1 - <M^4>/(3<M^2>^2) and helicity modulus, determine the following exponents: (1) nu: the perpendicular (in-plane) correlation-length exponent,     where xi_perp ~ |T - T_c|^{-nu}. (2) zeta: the anisotropy exponent defined by xi_z ~ xi_perp^{zeta}     (equivalently nu_parallel / nu_perp). (3) z_XY: the dynamic critical exponent for single-spin heat-bath Monte Carlo dynamics     (local phase-variable relaxational updates), defined by tau ~ xi_perp^{z_XY}. (4) z_loop: the dynamic critical exponent for elementary-vortex-loop heat-bath Monte Carlo     dynamics (loop updates of the dual model), defined by tau ~ xi_perp^{z_loop}. Report as a JSON object with keys "nu", "zeta", "z_XY", "z_loop". Each value should be a float.

metadata.system: 3D XY lattice model, simple cubic lattice, quenched columnar disorder (in-plane couplings from {0.1,1.9}, z-couplings=1)
metadata.source_excerpt: correlation length exponent is nu=1.2+/-0.1; anisotropy exponent zeta=1.3+/-0.1; dynamic exponent z_XY=2.6+/-0.1 (single-spin heat-bath); z_loop=3.4+/-0.1 (elementary-loop heat-bath, dual loop model).
metadata.difficulty_note: Columnar disorder gives new universality class; anisotropic FSS required. Both static and dynamic exponents. Two distinct dynamic universality classes.

question: Consider the three-dimensional lattice vortex-line model in the London limit (infinite bare magnetic penetration length lambda -> infinity, i.e. unscreened long-range vortex-vortex interactions) defined by the Hamiltonian H = (1/2) sum_{r,r'} V(r-r') q(r)·q(r'), where q(r) are integer vorticity vectors at each site, V(r) is the isotropic 3D long-range interaction, with quenched columnar defects having random core energies D(r_perp) drawn uniformly from [0, 0.8], coupling constant K=1, and quarter-integer vortex filling f=1/4. An anisotropic finite-size scaling (FSS) analysis uses aspect ratio L_z ~ L^{zeta} to separate perpendicular (xy-plane) and parallel (z-axis) scaling. From Monte Carlo FSS of the helicity modulus and linear resistivity, determine: (1) nu: the perpendicular correlation-length exponent, xi_perp ~ |T-T_c|^{-nu}. (2) zeta: the anisotropy exponent, xi_z ~ xi_perp^{zeta} (= nu_parallel/nu_perp). (3) z: the dynamic critical exponent for local single-plaquette loop-insertion Monte Carlo     dynamics, defined by tau ~ xi_perp^z. Report as a JSON object with keys "nu", "zeta", "z". Each value should be a float.

metadata.system: 3D lattice vortex-line model, unscreened (lambda->inf), columnar disorder D in [0,0.8], filling f=1/4
metadata.source_excerpt: perpendicular correlation-length exponent nu=1.0+/-0.1; anisotropy exponent zeta=1.25+/-0.1 (xi_z ~ xi^zeta); dynamic exponent z=1.95+/-0.1 for local single-plaquette loop-insertion dynamics.
metadata.difficulty_note: Unscreened (long-range) vortex universality class; distinct from screened model. Anisotropic FSS. Model is London-limit lattice gauge theory.

question: Consider the three-dimensional Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass on a simple cubic lattice with nearest-neighbor bimodal couplings J_{ij} in {+1, -1} (drawn with equal probability) and periodic boundary conditions. The Hamiltonian is H = -sum_{<ij>} J_{ij} s_i s_j. Finite-size scaling (FSS) analysis employs Privman-Fisher scaling of the thermodynamic-limit susceptibility chi(tau) and the normalized second-moment correlation length xi(tau)/beta, where tau = 1 - (beta/beta_c)^2 is the reduced temperature variable and beta_c = 0.9075 is the critical inverse temperature. Log-log Wegner scaling fits over the full paramagnetic regime (system sizes L=6,10,20,32) yield the static critical exponents. Report the following as a JSON object: "nu" (correlation-length exponent, xi ~ |T-T_c|^{-nu}), "gamma" (susceptibility exponent, chi ~ |T-T_c|^{-gamma}). Each value should be a float.

metadata.system: 3D Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass, simple cubic, bimodal couplings J_{ij}=±1, periodic BC
metadata.source_excerpt: Privman-Fisher scaling plots for both observables show excellent collapse for all system sizes L=6,10,20,32 over the full temperature range, with critical exponents gamma = 6.15(10) and nu = 2.45(10) that are consistent with independent finite-size scaling analyses.
metadata.difficulty_note: 3D Ising spin glass; large nu value (nu~2.45) and large gamma (~6.15) characteristic of spin glass universality. nu is cited from literature but confirmed by this paper's Privman-Fisher analysis.

question: Consider the two-dimensional Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass on a square lattice with nearest-neighbor couplings J_{xy} drawn from a Gaussian distribution (zero mean, unit variance) and periodic boundary conditions. The Hamiltonian is H = -sum_{<xy>} J_{xy} s_x s_y. Finite-size scaling (FSS) is performed near T=0 where criticality occurs. The spin overlap is q_x = s_x tau_x (product over two independent replicas with the same couplings), the Binder ratio is U_4 = overline{<q^4>} / overline{<q^2>}^2, and the dimensionless ratio xi_L/L is used as the FSS control variable. Renormalized temperature quotients Q_T^R(L) are used to extract the thermal exponent 1/nu. The overlap correlation function C(r) ~ 1/r^{D-2+eta} (D=2) at short distances gives eta. Report the following as a JSON object: "nu" (correlation-length exponent, the inverse of the thermal exponent 1/nu you extract from the renormalized temperature quotients Q_T^R(L)), "eta" (anomalous dimension of the overlap-overlap correlation function), "omega" (leading corrections-to-scaling exponent from FSS fits of U_4). Each value should be a float.

metadata.system: 2D Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass, square lattice, Gaussian couplings N(0,1), periodic BC
metadata.source_excerpt: thermal critical exponent 1/nu = 0.283(6) (equivalently nu~3.53); anomalous dimension eta = 0.00(2), conservative bound |eta|<0.02; leading corrections exponent omega = 0.75(10)(5).
metadata.difficulty_note: 2D spin glass at T=0 critical point; very large nu (~3.53). Eta~0 is a non-trivial result. Multispin-coding implementation.

question: Consider the three-dimensional Ising model on a simple cubic lattice with quenched nonmagnetic impurities arranged as mutually parallel linear defects running along the z-axis (impurity concentration p=0.2, meaning 20% of sites along each z-column are nonmagnetic). The system exhibits anisotropic critical scaling with distinct perpendicular (perp, in the xy-plane) and parallel (||, along z) correlation-length exponents: xi_perp ~ |t|^{-nu_perp} and xi_|| ~ |t|^{-nu_||}, where t = (T-T_c)/T_c. The anisotropy ratio theta = nu_|| / nu_perp satisfies 1.1 <= theta <= 1.3 as established by Binder-cumulant and correlation-length scaling. An anisotropic FSS study uses L_|| = L_perp^theta (rounded to nearest integer) for perpendicular sizes L_perp = 10..100, and 128 disorder realizations per size. The susceptibility chi = beta V (overline{<M^2> - <|M|>^2}) is fitted as chi ~ L_perp^b at the pseudocritical inverse temperature beta_max^A = 0.25655(10). Report the exponent ratio as a JSON object with key: "gamma_over_nu_perp" (ratio gamma/nu_perp, where gamma is the susceptibility exponent and nu_perp is the perpendicular correlation-length exponent). Use the value averaged over the theta range [1.1, 1.3]. Provide a float.

metadata.system: 3D Ising model, simple cubic, parallel linear defects along z, impurity concentration p=0.2
metadata.source_excerpt: using anisotropic FSS fits of the susceptibility at beta_max^A=0.25655(10) with L_||=L_perp^theta for 1.1<=theta<=1.3, the fitted exponent b yields gamma/nu_perp ~= 1.85 +/- 0.05 (averaged over theta window). For theta=1.1 (favored by Binder analysis): gamma/nu_perp ~= 1.90 +/- 0.08.
metadata.difficulty_note: Anisotropic FSS; result depends on choice of theta. Only gamma/nu_perp extracted (not nu_perp separately). Compare to RG estimates ~2.0.
